skill.name: azure-web-pubsub-ts
skill.description: Build TypeScript applications with Azure Web PubSub, including server messaging, client connections, groups, tokens, and Express event handlers.
skill.summary: Use Azure Web PubSub SDKs to add real-time messaging to TypeScript applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many shell execution patterns, but these are Markdown code fences and TypeScript examples, not executable skill logic. The skill is generally publishable, but it includes examples that access environment secrets and log a client access token URL, so users need security warnings.

medium_findings.title: Client access token logging example
medium_findings.description: The example logs token.url after generating a Web PubSub client access token. That URL contains an access token and could expose credentials if copied into production logging.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly logs the generated token URL, and the inline comment shows the URL contains access_token. This is a real security anti-pattern, though it appears instructional rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Environment secret access is instructional
low_findings.description: The examples read WEBPUBSUB_CONNECTION_STRING and WEBPUBSUB_ENDPOINT from process.env. This is normal Azure SDK configuration, but users should avoid exposing connection strings in logs or client code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The env access is clearly tied to Azure SDK authentication examples. It is legitimate, but it handles sensitive configuration values.

low_findings.title: Network examples use expected Azure and negotiate endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are placeholder Azure Web PubSub endpoints, and the fetch call targets a local negotiate route. No evidence found of unauthorized external exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match documented Azure Web PubSub configuration and the fetch path is relative. I did not find evidence that secrets are sent to an unexpected host.

low_findings.title: External command detections are Markdown examples
low_findings.description: The detected shell execution locations are Markdown code fences and npm install examples. No executable scripts, hooks, or tool instructions run these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows the findings point to fenced Markdown blocks, not Ruby backticks or executable code. This is a static analyzer false positive.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detection is a false positive
low_findings.description: The flagged line is the skill description mentioning Azure Web PubSub SDK packages. No evidence found of a weak cryptographic algorithm in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains prose and package names only. It does not contain cryptographic code or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: Critical heuristic is not confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential heuristic is not supported by semantic review. The file is documentation with TypeScript examples and no hidden execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only reviewed file is a Markdown skill guide. I found no prompt injection, obfuscation, malicious network destination, or executable automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Access token appears in console output
dangerous_patterns.description: The token generation example prints the client access URL. Production code should return the token only to the authenticated client that requested it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example directly prints a token-bearing URL. This is dangerous if reused in application logging.

content.user_title: Build Azure Web PubSub TypeScript Apps
content.value_statement: Real-time messaging requires secure WebSocket setup, token handling, and group routing. This skill gives TypeScript patterns for Azure Web PubSub servers and clients.
actual_capabilities: Shows npm packages for Azure Web PubSub server, client, identity, and Express middleware.
Demonstrates service client authentication with connection strings, DefaultAzureCredential, and AzureKeyCredential.
Explains client access token generation with users, roles, groups, and expiration settings.
Provides TypeScript examples for broadcasting, user messages, connection messages, and group messages.
Covers WebPubSubClient connection setup, group sending, lifecycle handlers, and reconnect events.
Includes an Express event handler pattern for connect validation, user events, and negotiate endpoints.
limitations: It is a reference skill and does not provision Azure resources.
It does not include complete application scaffolding or deployment automation.
Security examples need review before production use, especially token logging.
It assumes users already understand TypeScript, Express, and Azure project setup.

use_cases.title: Add Chat Channels
use_cases.description: Use group APIs and client handlers to build room-based chat with Azure Web PubSub.

use_cases.title: Create Live Notifications
use_cases.description: Broadcast server events to connected users through hub, user, group, or connection targets.

use_cases.title: Secure WebSocket Negotiation
use_cases.description: Generate client access URLs from an authenticated backend and assign scoped roles and groups.

prompt_templates.title: Install the SDKs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Azure Web PubSub TypeScript skill to list the npm packages I need for server, client, and Express integration.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Client Connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Azure Web PubSub TypeScript skill to draft a client connection flow that gets a URL from a negotiate endpoint and registers handlers before start.

prompt_templates.title: Design Group Messaging
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Azure Web PubSub TypeScript skill to design group chat messaging with join, send, leave, and reconnect handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Token Security
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Azure Web PubSub TypeScript skill to review my negotiate endpoint for token scope, expiration, authentication, and logging risks.

output_examples.input: Build a basic Azure Web PubSub chat flow.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering SDK installation, server client setup, token negotiation, client connection, group join, and message handlers.

output_examples.input: Review my Web PubSub permissions approach.
output: Check that each token has the minimum required roles.
Use short token expiration times for browser clients.
Avoid logging token-bearing URLs or connection strings.

output_examples.input: Explain how to route messages to users and groups.
output_examples.output: A comparison of broadcast, user, connection, and group messaging, with guidance on when each target is appropriate.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential for production services when possible.
Register client event handlers before starting the WebPubSubClient.
Validate users in connect handlers before assigning groups or roles.
anti_patterns: Do not log client access URLs because they include bearer-style access tokens.
Do not place Web PubSub connection strings in browser code.
Do not grant broad group permissions when a scoped role is sufficient.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It explains TypeScript SDK usage after an Azure Web PubSub resource already exists.

faq.question: Can it help with browser clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers WebPubSubClient setup, dynamic access URLs, group messages, and lifecycle events.

faq.question: Can it help with backend messaging?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows service client patterns for broadcasting and sending messages to users, groups, and connections.

faq.question: Does it cover authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows connection strings, Azure identity, access keys, and connect handler validation patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to copy every example into production?
faq.answer: No. Review token logging, credential handling, authentication, and permission scope before production use.

faq.question: Does it include Express support?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an Express event handler pattern for connect events, user events, and negotiation.
